title: async and await

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific JavaScript: Language and Browser Applications draft for async and await; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: ECMAScript Language Specification; living specification accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to async and await
sources.claim: async functions return promises, and await pauses only that async continuation while other tasks can proceed.

sources.claim: async functions return promises, and await pauses only that async continuation while other tasks can proceed.

canonicalExplanation.summary: async functions return promises, and await pauses only that async continuation while other tasks can proceed.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: Two independent fetch promises start before Promise.all is awaited, avoiding unnecessary serialization. Your goal is to refactor a promise workflow using async/await and justify its concurrency behavior. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Call stack and event loop model; Closures and lifetime; Promises and chains; Parameters, rest and return contracts. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: async functions return promises, and await pauses only that async continuation while other tasks can proceed. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Sequential awaits can serialize independent work, try/catch scope matters, and rejection still needs handling. This draft therefore rejects: await blocks the browser thread until the promise settles.

includes: Write structured asynchronous functions and distinguish sequential from intentional concurrent waits.
Refactor a promise workflow using async/await and justify its concurrency behavior.
excludes: Sequential awaits can serialize independent work, try/catch scope matters, and rejection still needs handling.

outcomes: Refactor a promise workflow using async/await and justify its concurrency behavior.

examples.title: async and await: concrete case
examples.situation: Two independent fetch promises start before Promise.all is awaited, avoiding unnecessary serialization.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: Two independent fetch promises start before Promise.all is awaited, avoiding unnecessary serialization.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: await suspends the async function continuation, not the entire event loop.
examples.result: The result follows because await suspends the async function continuation, not the entire event loop.

examples.situation: Sequential awaits can serialize independent work, try/catch scope matters, and rejection still needs handling.

Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Reject the misconception: await blocks the browser thread until the promise settles.
examples.result: The boundary requires this correction: await suspends the async function continuation, not the entire event loop.

analogy.example: A coordinated event desk can model async and await, with values, tasks, callbacks, and external messages changing observable state.
usefulFor: Building a first mental model for async and await before using exact platform terms
limitations: Human coordination does not reproduce coercion, lexical scope, microtasks, prototypes, or browser security boundaries.
